3: The long-term observations and measurements of the earth temperature reveal that an increase of the temperature worldwide may be observed in the coming years. This increase may be corre- lated to the increase of the greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions caused by human-related activities. Besides, the increase of the GHG con- centrations in the earth atmosphere of the recent period exceeds even significantly the values which are typical for the interglacial periods [1]. Therefore, politicians and decision makers worldwide have decided to seek for less environmentally polluting solutions and thus the promotion of a climate friendly lifestyle. One focus has been put especially on finding environmentally acceptable ways of energy provision in the form of renewable energy sources, what could substitute and hence diminish the impact of fossil fuel combustion.

In 2000, Germany has implemented a legal act aiming at the in- crease of the share of renewable energies on the German electricity market, namely the Renewable Energy Sources Act (EEG). The EEG is a feed-in tariff system that allows the producers to feed in renewable electricity into the national grid at a guaranteed price

over 20 years. This act can be considered as one of the most impor- tant milestones in the development of the green energy market in Germany and has successfully facilitated an increasing share of renewable energy in the overall electrical energy consumption. As for the year 2010 the share of renewables of the final electricity consumption amounted to 17%[2]. Thereby, most of the renewable electricity is produced by wind farms; biomass is the second most important source of renewable energy.
This paper presents a comparative analysis of twelve different bioenergy conversion pathways - gaseous, liquid, solid - for elec- tricity generation aiming at answering the following questions:

<list>(a) Which conversion pathways cause the smallest environ- mental impacts with respect to GHG emissions and other LCA impact categories (cumulative energy demand, acidifi- cation, and eutrophication)? 
(b) Which bioenergy options are most cost-effective? 
(c) Which bioenergy option has the lowest CO2 abatement costs?</list>
* Corresponding author. E-mail addresses: Christiane.Hennig@dbfz.de Hennig), Marek.Gawor@dbfz.de (M. Gawor).

The chosen bioenergy conversion pathways are representative and already established on the German market for the provision of electricity. However, technology improvements and changing market conditions require a periodic review of the modelling results of the described pathways, provided by this paper. For

comparing the GHG emissions and determining the CO2 abatement costs of the different options a fossil fuel plant and the German electricity mix as reference systems have been taken into consider- ation. The answers to the above-mentioned questions will be given in the following sections.

However, technology improvements and changing market conditions require a periodic review of the modelling results of the described pathways, provided by this paper. For

comparing the GHG emissions and determining the CO2 abatement costs of the different options a fossil fuel plant and the German electricity mix as reference systems have been taken into consider- ation. The answers to the above-mentioned questions will be given in the following sections.
6: The environmental analysis of the bioenergy options was per- formed by using the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) technique. The evaluation was made for the entire technological chain, starting from raw material acquisition (cultivation/preparation of the bio- mass, production of additional materials such as fertilizer), gener- ation of the process energy, through transportation and energy conversion of the biomass, and finally the disposal of the residues. Furthermore, the following assumptions have been made:

The LCA modelling is based on the ISO 14,040 ff. standards [3,4], as well as the EU Directive 2009/28/EC [5]. The ISO standards de- fine the framework for performing the LCA, including the manda- tory and optional steps of the entire process as well as specific phases of the modelling (i.e. goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment, interpretation). The ISO standards al- low practically any modelling configuration, when the prerequisite of the transparency of the result description is fulfilled. The EU Directive bases on the rules of the ISO standards; however, it nar- rows the allowed methodology framework (i.e. for the biofuel pathways it is not allowed to apply credits for the avoided emis- sions, if the avoided product is not directly linked to the product system in question).

<list>The environmental analysis focuses on the emissions of green- house gases (using IPCC 2007 values for the Global Warming Potentials of the gases emitted during the life cycle of the respective bioenergy carrier), cumulative non-renewable energy demand (measuring the energy consumption based on the fossil fuels), as well as acidification and eutrophication potentials (parameters significant for the ecosystem quality and to some extent for human health). 
For each category of biomass (solid, liquid and gaseous) four installations were modelled, representing a range from 100 kW to 7.5 MW of electrical output. Within the gaseous bio- mass pathways three biogas plants using maize silage or a mix out of maize silage and manure as feedstock and one installa- tion using biodegradable waste for the biogas production which is based on anaerobic digestion are considered. The generated biogas is then combusted in CHP (combined heat and power) installations or further purified to bio-methane; within the liquid biomass two CHP plants using palm oil (150 and 200 MWel) and two CHP plants rapeseed oil (100 and 150 MWel) are taken into consideration; and within the solid biomass path- ways two CHP installations (an organic rankine cycle process 1 MWel and a gasification process 3 MWe1) using forest residues, one facility (a steam power process 4 MWe1) applying a mix out of forest residues and waste wood and one facility (a steam power process 7.5 MWe1) using short rotation coppice are con- sidered. The selection of a wide range of feedstock and various electrical output classes allows a comprehensive analysis. How- ever, for further research it is advisable to investigate even a lar- ger number of installations in order to better confirm the findings. The installations presented here are also considered in the following economic analysis. 
The modelling data is taken from various data bases (i.e. ecoin- vent) and literature sources as well as from current research activities of the German Biomass Research Centre (DBFZ).</list>

<list>The residues of the biogas fermentation process are distributed on the agricultural fields as a natural fertilizer, thus reducing the necessity of artificial fertilizer usage. This reduction was directly incorporated in the modules of the maize cultivation or accounted as an additional credit (biodegradable waste pathway). 
The methane losses of the biogas installations are set to 1% (dif- fuse emissions) and to 0.5% (biogas installation itself) according to [6]. 
The methane emissions of the palm oil mills were reduced due to the application of air-tight covers; the production of rapeseed oil is assumed to take place in Germany. 
Two different allocation methods are used for the alignment of the environmental effects to the heat and electricity provision, namely energetic allocation and exergetic allocation. The appli- cation of these two methods may give an overview of the possible differences in the results when applying various calcu- lation methods (compare [7]). 
As reference values for the environmental impacts a new coal fired power plant [8-10] and the German electricity mix are chosen [11-13].</list>

The investigated installations generate simultaneously electri- cal and thermal energy, with the main product electricity among the considered pathways. Therefore, an allocation procedure for the obtained results is necessary in order to distinguish between and define the environmental burden related to the electricity and heat production. In this study, two allocation procedures were applied, which are either based on the amount of energy generated (energetic allocation) or refer to the thermodynamic concept of exergy (exergetic allocation).

The next section of the article presents the LCA results of the impact categories of the GHG emissions, cumulative energy de- mand, acidification and eutrophication. Beforehand, it has to be mentioned that it is not possible to make a straightforward com- parison between those different impact categories. There is no di- rect relationship between i.e. high GHG emissions and high cumulative energy demand. Such relationships are dependent on many factors, i.e. type of technology used or geographical location. However, in many cases, the higher consumption of the non- renewable energy sources (i.e. fuel in the agricultural machinery)

leads to higher GHG emissions. Also applying larger amounts of fertilizer increases the GHG emissions (through the N20 field emis- sions) and may lead to intensified local eutrophication problems. The qualification and quantification of such relationships lies, how- ever, outside the scope of this paper.

The next section of the article presents the LCA results of the impact categories of the GHG emissions, cumulative energy de- mand, acidification and eutrophication. Beforehand, it has to be mentioned that it is not possible to make a straightforward com- parison between those different impact categories. There is no di- rect relationship between i.e. high GHG emissions and high cumulative energy demand. Such relationships are dependent on many factors, i.e. type of technology used or geographical location. However, in many cases, the higher consumption of the non- renewable energy sources (i.e. fuel in the agricultural machinery)

leads to higher GHG emissions. Also applying larger amounts of fertilizer increases the GHG emissions (through the N20 field emis- sions) and may lead to intensified local eutrophication problems. The qualification and quantification of such relationships lies, how- ever, outside the scope of this paper.
9: The environmental analysis of the bioenergy options was per- formed by using the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) technique. The evaluation was made for the entire technological chain, starting from raw material acquisition (cultivation/preparation of the bio- mass, production of additional materials such as fertilizer), gener- ation of the process energy, through transportation and energy conversion of the biomass, and finally the disposal of the residues. Furthermore, the following assumptions have been made:

The LCA modelling is based on the ISO 14,040 ff. standards [3,4], as well as the EU Directive 2009/28/EC [5]. The ISO standards de- fine the framework for performing the LCA, including the manda- tory and optional steps of the entire process as well as specific phases of the modelling (i.e. goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment, interpretation). The ISO standards al- low practically any modelling configuration, when the prerequisite of the transparency of the result description is fulfilled. The EU Directive bases on the rules of the ISO standards; however, it nar- rows the allowed methodology framework (i.e. for the biofuel pathways it is not allowed to apply credits for the avoided emis- sions, if the avoided product is not directly linked to the product system in question).

<list>The environmental analysis focuses on the emissions of green- house gases (using IPCC 2007 values for the Global Warming Potentials of the gases emitted during the life cycle of the respective bioenergy carrier), cumulative non-renewable energy demand (measuring the energy consumption based on the fossil fuels), as well as acidification and eutrophication potentials (parameters significant for the ecosystem quality and to some extent for human health). 
For each category of biomass (solid, liquid and gaseous) four installations were modelled, representing a range from 100 kW to 7.5 MW of electrical output. Within the gaseous bio- mass pathways three biogas plants using maize silage or a mix out of maize silage and manure as feedstock and one installa- tion using biodegradable waste for the biogas production which is based on anaerobic digestion are considered. The generated biogas is then combusted in CHP (combined heat and power) installations or further purified to bio-methane; within the liquid biomass two CHP plants using palm oil (150 and 200 MWel) and two CHP plants rapeseed oil (100 and 150 MWel) are taken into consideration; and within the solid biomass path- ways two CHP installations (an organic rankine cycle process 1 MWel and a gasification process 3 MWe1) using forest residues, one facility (a steam power process 4 MWe1) applying a mix out of forest residues and waste wood and one facility (a steam power process 7.5 MWe1) using short rotation coppice are con- sidered. The selection of a wide range of feedstock and various electrical output classes allows a comprehensive analysis. How- ever, for further research it is advisable to investigate even a lar- ger number of installations in order to better confirm the findings. The installations presented here are also considered in the following economic analysis. 
The modelling data is taken from various data bases (i.e. ecoin- vent) and literature sources as well as from current research activities of the German Biomass Research Centre (DBFZ).</list>

The next section of the article presents the LCA results of the impact categories of the GHG emissions, cumulative energy de- mand, acidification and eutrophication. Beforehand, it has to be mentioned that it is not possible to make a straightforward com- parison between those different impact categories. There is no di- rect relationship between i.e. high GHG emissions and high cumulative energy demand. Such relationships are dependent on many factors, i.e. type of technology used or geographical location. However, in many cases, the higher consumption of the non- renewable energy sources (i.e. fuel in the agricultural machinery)

leads to higher GHG emissions. Also applying larger amounts of fertilizer increases the GHG emissions (through the N20 field emis- sions) and may lead to intensified local eutrophication problems. The qualification and quantification of such relationships lies, how- ever, outside the scope of this paper.

The LCA modelling is based on the ISO 14,040 ff. standards [3,4], as well as the EU Directive 2009/28/EC [5]. The ISO standards de- fine the framework for performing the LCA, including the manda- tory and optional steps of the entire process as well as specific phases of the modelling (i.e. goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment, interpretation). The ISO standards al- low practically any modelling configuration, when the prerequisite of the transparency of the result description is fulfilled. For each category of biomass (solid, liquid and gaseous) four installations were modelled, representing a range from 100 kW to 7.5 MW of electrical output. The selection of a wide range of feedstock and various electrical output classes allows a comprehensive analysis. How- ever, for further research it is advisable to investigate even a lar- ger number of installations in order to better confirm the findings. The generated biogas is then combusted in CHP (combined heat and power) installations or further purified to bio-methane; within the liquid biomass two CHP plants using palm oil (150 and 200 MWel) and two CHP plants rapeseed oil (100 and 150 MWel) are taken into consideration; and within the solid biomass path- ways two CHP installations (an organic rankine cycle process 1 MWel and a gasification process 3 MWe1) using forest residues, one facility (a steam power process 4 MWe1) applying a mix out of forest residues and waste wood and one facility (a steam power process 7.5 MWe1) using short rotation coppice are con- sidered. The selection of a wide range of feedstock and various electrical output classes allows a comprehensive analysis. How- ever, for further research it is advisable to investigate even a lar- ger number of installations in order to better confirm the findings. The installations presented here are also considered in the following economic analysis. 
The modelling data is taken from various data bases (i.e. ecoin- vent) and literature sources as well as from current research activities of the German Biomass Research Centre (DBFZ).</list>

<list>The residues of the biogas fermentation process are distributed on the agricultural fields as a natural fertilizer, thus reducing the necessity of artificial fertilizer usage. This reduction was directly incorporated in the modules of the maize cultivation or accounted as an additional credit (biodegradable waste pathway). 
The methane losses of the biogas installations are set to 1% (dif- fuse emissions) and to 0.5% (biogas installation itself) according to [6]. 
The methane emissions of the palm oil mills were reduced due to the application of air-tight covers; the production of rapeseed oil is assumed to take place in Germany. 
Two different allocation methods are used for the alignment of the environmental effects to the heat and electricity provision, namely energetic allocation and exergetic allocation. The appli- cation of these two methods may give an overview of the possible differences in the results when applying various calcu- lation methods (compare [7]). 
As reference values for the environmental impacts a new coal fired power plant [8-10] and the German electricity mix are chosen [11-13].</list>

The investigated installations generate simultaneously electri- cal and thermal energy, with the main product electricity among the considered pathways. Therefore, an allocation procedure for the obtained results is necessary in order to distinguish between and define the environmental burden related to the electricity and heat production. In this study, two allocation procedures were applied, which are either based on the amount of energy generated (energetic allocation) or refer to the thermodynamic concept of exergy (exergetic allocation).

The next section of the article presents the LCA results of the impact categories of the GHG emissions, cumulative energy de- mand, acidification and eutrophication. Beforehand, it has to be mentioned that it is not possible to make a straightforward com- parison between those different impact categories. There is no di- rect relationship between i.e. high GHG emissions and high cumulative energy demand. Such relationships are dependent on many factors, i.e. type of technology used or geographical location. However, in many cases, the higher consumption of the non- renewable energy sources (i.e. fuel in the agricultural machinery)

leads to higher GHG emissions. Also applying larger amounts of fertilizer increases the GHG emissions (through the N20 field emis- sions) and may lead to intensified local eutrophication problems. The qualification and quantification of such relationships lies, how- ever, outside the scope of this paper.
17: The detailed results of the GHG emissions are presented in Table 3 and Fig. 2. As the results show, all biomass installations produce electricity with much smaller GHG emissions than a con- ventional coal power plant or in comparison to the current electric- ity mix in Germany. Furthermore, the differences between similar biofuels are relatively low, especially in case of the categories li- quid and solid biofuels. However, significant differences may be noticed when various allocation methodologies are used or the dif- ferent feedstock types (i.e. wood VS. palm oil) are compared.

In general solid biofuels (wood) cause very small GHG emission, as they do not require the fertilizer input. In the case of the short rotation coppice, though, some fertilization is necessary, which influences the final result. The comparison of the liquid and gas- eous biofuels shall be made separately on the basis of the mini- mum and maximum values. The minimum values reveal, that electricity production from biogas causes smaller GHG emissions than electricity produced from liquid biofuels. However, if the maximum values are taken into consideration, liquid biofuel con- version produces less greenhouse gases. The reason for such a dif- ference may be associated with the scenario of upgrading and purifying of biogas to bio-methane quality (and subsequent feed-in of the bio-methane into the biogas grid). The additional methane emissions from the upgrading process cause an increase of the emissions in terms of CO2-equivalent results. Currently,

25: The acidifying and eutrophication effect of the bioenergy gener- ation depends strongly on the amount of chemicals (in the form of fuel or fertilizer) used along the entire process chain. Thus, acidifi- cation and eutrophication are especially high in the case of the gas- eous (fertilizer used) and liquid biofuels (fertilizer use - rapeseed oil, transportation - palm oil) pathways as shown in Figs. 4 and 5. The 190 kW biogas plant has the highest acidification/eutrophica- tion potential, as it shows a lower efficiency in comparison to the other installations. The wooden biofuels create the smallest envi- ronmental burden in this impact category. However, the use of the fertilization in the short rotation plantations may also worsen the results. The values of the acidification/eutrophication potentials of the selected conversion pathways are in the same range as for the fossil references (compare Figs. 4 and 5). Although one could expect worse values for the fossil energy generation it has to be mentioned, that the modern coal power plants are equipped with filters and ex- haust gas cleaning systems reducing the emissions of substances causing acidification and eutrophication significantly. The monitor- ing of the emissions in case of bioenergy feedstock is more difficult due to their diffuse character.
26: Following the life cycle analysis the chosen bioenergy conver- sion pathways are examined under economic aspects by consider- ing on the one hand the electricity production costs and on the other hand the profitability expressed as gain or loss by operating a specific bioenergy plant. The calculation of the profitability is based on the annuity method (a dynamic investment calculation) where the net present value of an investment is equally distributed over the lifetime of an investment project. The result is regular and equal payments per period over the considered lifetime, which is called annuity. An annuity can be positive or negative, i.e. an

average annual gain or loss per year for carrying out a certain pro- ject. Thus, an investment should be considered as feasible when its annuity is positive, i.e. the amount invested plus the interest rate paid are received back [14]. With respect to bioenergy generation it means the average yearly gain or loss of operating a bioenergy plant e.g. for electricity (and heat) provision.

The electricity production costs are determined by dividing the sum of total annual costs (in including capital-related (invest- ment), consumption-related (feedstock), operating-related (main- tenance) and other costs by the annual electricity production (in kW h). By subtracting the total annual costs from the total annual revenues the value of the annuity is obtained [14]. In this research it is assumed that the total annual revenues consist of the compen- sation payment within the Renewable Energy Sources Act (EEG) of 2009 plus the revenues from heat sales cogenerated while the elec- tricity production. The level of the EEG 2009 compensation pay- ments mainly depends on the size of the plant, the technology as well as the fuel used and the amount of cogenerated heat produced (compare [15]). Fig. 6 gives an overview on the methodology for the calculation of the electricity production costs and the profit- ability of providing energy from biomass used within this study. For the calculations, the following basic assumptions are taken into account: All plants represented new installations which are operated over 20 years. The considered lifetime corresponds to the scope of the Renewable Energy Sources Act that provides com- pensation payment for 20 years (compare [15]). paid within the EEG over all considered bioenergy plants are summarised. Considering the solid biomass pathways the investment in the facilities represents the largest proportion in the total costs (40-50%). This is mainly due to the fact that the conversion technologies gasification and organic rankine cycle are more expensive technologies. On the one hand as a result of the immaturity of the technology and on the other hand as a result of the complexity of the technology.

The calculation of the profitability is based on the annuity method (a dynamic investment calculation) where the net present value of an investment is equally distributed over the lifetime of an investment project. The result is regular and equal payments per period over the considered lifetime, which is called annuity. An annuity can be positive or negative, i.e. an

average annual gain or loss per year for carrying out a certain pro- ject. Thus, an investment should be considered as feasible when its annuity is positive, i.e. the amount invested plus the interest rate paid are received back [14]. 32: In order to identify the favoured bioenergy provision pathway both from an environmental and an economic perspective the CO2 abatement costs are determined. CO2 abatement costs repre-

sent the costs that arise for reducing a certain amount of CO2 emissions by applying an alternative system in comparison to a reference system [16]. As a reference system a modern coal power plant is used1 as in the section environmental analysis. The electric- ity production cost and CO2 emissions of the considered coal power plant originate from a recent study that did prediction on those parameters for 2010 [8]. The Eq. (6) defines the calculation of CO2 abatement costs used within this research:

AC CO2 abatement costs (ect/CO2-eq); Calt. system - electricity produc- tion cost of an alternative system (Ect/kW hel); Cref. system - electricity production cost of a reference system (Ect/kW hel); Eref. system CO2 emissions of alternative system (gCO2-eq/kW) Ealt. fuel CO2 emissions of reference system (gCO2-eq/kWhei).

The lowest CO2 abatement costs are realised within the bioen- ergy conversion pathway BW 600 kWel. Thus, this installation rep- resents the most favourable one among the different pathways. An overview on all the CO2 abatement costs of the different bioenergy pathways is given in Fig. 8.

34: The environmental analysis using the LCA technique reveals that the highest level of emissions and environmental impacts is generated during the cultivation/provision phase of the bioenergy generation chain. Solid biofuels, such as waste wood and short rotation coppice, cause comparatively smaller environmental

1 In regards to the vegetable oil plants the more suitable reference system concerning the mode of operation is a gas-fired power plant. However, due to a holistic approach for the evaluation of the different bioenergy pathways only one reference system has been chosen.

0: <title>C. Hennig, M. Gawor/Energy Conversion and Management 63 (2012) 130-137 </title>

Fig. 8. CO2 abatement costs of the investigated bioenergy pathways, MS - maize silage, M - manure, BW - biodegradable waste, RO - rapeseed oil, PO - palm oil, FR - forest residues, WW - waste wood, SRC - short rotation coppice.

damages than liquid or gaseous biofuels. All bioenergy carriers, however, offer a significant emissions reduction potential in comparison to the current electricity mix in Germany or fossil fuels as reference systems. Furthermore, the obtained result strongly de- pends on the type of calculation methodology used (i.e. applied allocation procedure). Moreover, the results expressed only in terms of CO2-eq emissions may not be fully representative for the entire environmental effects and thus shall not be used as the only criterion in specifying, whether a bioenergy pathway is environmentally friendly and sustainable. Summarizing the results from the economic analysis, the solid biomass and the gaseous pathways show the lowest costs of elec- tricity generation. Overall capital- and consumption-related costs have the largest share within the total costs. Hence, prospective learning curves in the technology development can result in lower electricity production costs, and marginal changes in the consump- tion-related costs (mainly market prices for biofuels) can influence the profitability significantly (leverage effect).

Hence, prospective learning curves in the technology development can result in lower electricity production costs, and marginal changes in the consump- tion-related costs (mainly market prices for biofuels) can influence the profitability significantly (leverage effect).
2: The provision of electricity from biomass resources in an eco- nomically feasible and at the same time environmentally friendly manner still presents a challenge. Most of the installations offer smaller environmental impacts than fossil fuel based installations. However, it has been noted that the minority of the different bio- energy conversion pathways is profitable. Thereby the use of gas- eous and solid biomass is economically more favourable than the use of liquid biofuels. Overall, electricity provision from biomass is still notably more expensive than e.g. from the reference a con- ventional coal power plant. Thus, the choice of a bioenergy conver- sion pathway for electricity production is very much dependent from a suitable concept of the technology and feedstock used as well as the size of the installation.
